The people are asked to explain why they were tired of worshiping God and inclined toward idolatry. Sin causes disagreement between God and humans. God reasons with us to teach us to examine ourselves. They should remember God’s many blessings to them and their ancestors, and compare those blessings with their unworthy and ungrateful behavior towards him.

These verses seem to contain the main points of Balak's consultation with Balaam about how to gain the favor of Israel's God. A deep sense of guilt and anger will lead people to carefully seek peace and forgiveness, and then there may be some reason for hope regarding them. For God to be pleased with us, our focus must be on having a share in Christ’s sacrifice, so that the sin by which we displease him can be removed. What will satisfy God's justice? In whose name must we come, since we have nothing of our own to offer? In what righteousness shall we appear before him? The proposals reveal ignorance, though they show enthusiasm. They offer something very rich and costly. Those who are fully convinced of sin, and of their misery and danger because of it, would give all the world, if they had it, for peace and forgiveness. Yet they do not offer correctly. The sacrifices were valuable because they referred to Christ; it was impossible that the blood of bulls and goats could take away sin. And all proposals for peace, except those according to the gospel, are absurd. They cannot satisfy God’s justice, nor atone for the wrong done to the honor of God by sin, nor would they serve in place of holiness of heart and reformation of life. People will give up anything rather than their sins; but they do not give up what is needed to be accepted by God unless they give up their sins. Moral duties are commanded because they are good for people. There is a great reward in keeping God’s commandments, both during and after doing so. God has not only made it known, but also made it clear. The good that God requires of us is not paying a price for the forgiveness of sin and acceptance with God, but love for himself; and what is unreasonable or difficult about this? Every thought within us must be brought down to obedience to God if we want to live comfortably with him. We must do this as repentant sinners, depending on the Redeemer and his sacrifice. Blessed be the Lord that he is always ready to give his grace to humble, waiting penitents.

God, having shown how necessary it was for them to act justly, now shows how clear it was that they had acted unjustly. This voice of the Lord says to everyone, listen to the rod when it is coming, before you see it and feel it. Listen to the rod when it has come, and you are aware of the pain; listen to what advice and warnings it speaks. The voice of God is to be heard in the rod of God. Those who are dishonest in their dealings will never be considered pure, no matter what displays of devotion they make. What is gained through fraud and oppression cannot be kept or enjoyed with satisfaction. What we hold closest, we commonly lose soonest. Sin is a root of bitterness, quickly planted but not quickly uprooted. Their being the people of God in name and profession, while maintaining their relationship with him, was an honor to them; but now, as backsliders, their having once been the people of God becomes a source of shame.
